h1Title: Azoospermia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Azoospermia is the complete absence of sperm in the ejaculate confirmed on two separate semen analyses performed at least 7 days apart, and represents the most severe form of male-factor infertility. Infection-related azoospermia develops when pathogens — most commonly Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, or mumps virus — damage the epididymis, vas deferens, or testicular tubular architecture sufficiently to block sperm transport or destroy spermatogenesis. Azoospermia affects approximately 1% of all men and 10-15% of men evaluated for infertility (WHO 2021 laboratory manual). Infection is one of the most preventable causes: prompt treatment of bacterial STIs before epididymal obstruction occurs can preserve fertility. Surgical sperm retrieval combined with assisted reproductive technology achieves biological fatherhood in the majority of carefully selected men.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1% of all men and 10-15% of men evaluated for infertility; infection accounts for roughly 15-20% of obstructive azoospermia cases
keyStats.demographics: Reproductive-age men aged 20-45; highest risk in men with history of untreated STIs, mumps orchitis, or genitourinary tuberculosis
keyStats.avgAge: Typically diagnosed during infertility investigation in the third to fourth decade; underlying infection may have occurred 5-20 years earlier
keyStats.globalCases: WHO estimates 48.5 million couples are affected by infertility globally; male factor is involved in approximately 50% of cases; infection-related azoospermia most prevalent in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia where STI rates are highest

definition: Azoospermia due to infection (ICD-10: N46.022) is complete absence of sperm in semen caused by genitourinary infections that damage the male reproductive tract. Two pathophysiological mechanisms explain infection-related azoospermia. Obstructive azoospermia occurs when inflammation and scarring from epididymitis (typically chlamydial or gonococcal), vas deferens infection, or ejaculatory duct obstruction physically blocks sperm transport from the testis to the ejaculatory duct; spermatogenesis is intact but sperm cannot exit. Non-obstructive azoospermia results from direct testicular damage — viral orchitis from mumps (with a 30-40% rate of subsequent spermatogenic failure in severe bilateral cases) or granulomatous orchitis from Mycobacterium tuberculosis destroying the seminiferous tubules. The distinction between obstructive and non-obstructive subtypes is critical because it determines treatment: obstructive cases are potentially curable by surgical reconstruction, whereas non-obstructive cases require sperm retrieval from the testis or remain infertile. The condition is managed by urological andrologists or reproductive medicine specialists working alongside embryologists offering assisted reproductive technologies.

typesClassification.description: Bilateral epididymal obstruction from chlamydial or gonococcal epididymitis is the most common infectious cause of OA in younger men in high-income countries. Scarring from STIs blocks the epididymal duct; testicular function and sperm production are normal. Post-vasectomy obstructions are excluded from this category. Vasoepididymostomy (surgical reconnection) or sperm retrieval is curative.

Orchitis from mumps virus or, less commonly, from other viral pathogens (Coxsackie, ECHO virus) can destroy the seminiferous epithelium. Bilateral mumps orchitis produces azoospermia in approximately 30-40% of severely affected men. Testicular volume is reduced, FSH is elevated, and sperm retrieval rates at TESE are 30-50%.

typesClassification.type: Genitourinary tuberculosis causing ejaculatory duct obstruction
typesClassification.description: Mycobacterium tuberculosis can infect the epididymis, vas deferens, and seminal vesicles, producing extensive calcification and obstruction. Often asymptomatic in terms of urinary symptoms but presents with azoospermia and a dry ejaculate (hypospermia or aspermia) when seminal vesicle involvement reduces ejaculatory volume markedly.

primarySymptoms: Absence of sperm on semen analysis — the defining finding, often discovered during infertility investigation without any prior warning symptom.
Normal ejaculate volume (≥1.5 mL) in obstructive azoospermia, as seminal vesicles and prostate continue producing seminal plasma even when sperm transport is blocked.
Low ejaculate volume (<1.5 mL) and absent sperm in cases of seminal vesicle obstruction from tuberculosis or ejaculatory duct scarring, indicating distal duct involvement.
Normal testicular volume and consistency in obstructive azoospermia, reflecting intact spermatogenesis behind the obstruction.
Small, soft testes in non-obstructive azoospermia from mumps orchitis or other causes of tubular damage, indicating significant germ cell loss.
Absence of the vas deferens on scrotal palpation in congenital bilateral absence, which can follow severe gonococcal or tubercular destruction of the vas in rare cases.
Prior history of unilateral or bilateral orchitis — swelling, pain, and fever of the testicle — that may have occurred years before the fertility workup.
Normal serum testosterone and normal or mildly elevated FSH in obstructive azoospermia; markedly elevated FSH (above 7.6 IU/L) suggests primary testicular damage.
earlyWarningSigns: Unilateral or bilateral epididymal tenderness or nodularity in a young man with a history of STI — suggests epididymitis that could cause obstructive azoospermia if untreated.
Testicular pain, fever, and swelling 4-6 days after mumps parotitis in a post-pubertal male — mumps orchitis requiring monitoring for subsequent testicular atrophy.
Hematospermia or reduced ejaculatory volume in a man with a history of urogenital tuberculosis or recurrent STIs.
Palpable thickening or beading of the vas deferens in a man with a history of recurrent STIs or genitourinary tuberculosis.
emergencySigns: Bilateral testicular pain with swelling and fever in a post-pubertal male — bilateral orchitis from mumps or other viruses threatens future fertility and requires urgent andrological assessment.
Acute urinary retention or hematuria in a man with known genitourinary tuberculosis — suggests extensive upper tract involvement requiring urgent urological intervention.
Severe systemic sepsis in the context of bilateral epididymo-orchitis — uncommon but requires hospital admission for intravenous antibiotics to prevent irreversible testicular damage.

causes.cause: Chlamydia trachomatis epididymitis causing obstructive azoospermia
causes.description: The most common bacterial STI cause in reproductive-age men in high-income countries. Ascending chlamydial infection causes epididymitis, triggering an intense inflammatory and fibrotic response in the epididymal tubules. Bilateral involvement, even in successive episodes, can produce complete obstruction. Chlamydia accounts for approximately 50-60% of STI-related epididymitis in men under 35.

Gonococcal infection causes rapid-onset acute epididymo-orchitis with marked swelling and scrotal erythema. Scarring within the epididymis and vas deferens from repeated or undertreated gonococcal infection has historically been a major cause of azoospermia in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, where untreated gonorrhea remains prevalent.

Mumps virus (paramyxovirus) infects the testicular Sertoli and Leydig cells through hematogenous spread during mumps viremia. Post-pubertal orchitis develops in 30-40% of mumps cases in unvaccinated men; bilateral orchitis causes azoospermia in approximately 30-40% of those severely affected. MMR vaccination virtually eliminates this cause in vaccinated populations.

Mycobacterium tuberculosis spreads hematogenously to the epididymis, which is the most commonly affected male genital organ. Epididymal tuberculous granulomas and caseous necrosis produce bilateral obstruction of the epididymis and, in advanced cases, calcification and destruction of the vas deferens. Often presents as painless epididymal induration rather than acute infection.

causes.cause: Bacterial prostatitis, seminal vesiculitis, and ejaculatory duct obstruction
causes.description: Recurrent bacterial prostatitis or seminal vesiculitis from E. coli, Klebsiella, or Mycoplasma can produce ejaculatory duct obstruction through periductal fibrosis, reducing ejaculatory volume and blocking sperm egress. Reactive oxygen species generated during prostate inflammation can also impair spermatogenesis through oxidative damage.

causes.cause: Direct testicular infection by bacterial orchitis
causes.description: Pyogenic bacteria causing epididymo-orchitis can directly damage the testicular parenchyma if infection penetrates the tunica albuginea. Abscess formation within the testis, though rare, destroys the seminiferous tubules irreversibly in affected areas. Prompt surgical drainage of testicular abscess preserves residual function.

riskFactors.factor: History of STIs, especially chlamydia or gonorrhea
riskFactors.description: A history of even a single episode of chlamydial or gonococcal epididymitis raises the risk of obstructive azoospermia substantially. Prompt treatment within 7 days of onset reduces fibrotic scarring; delayed diagnosis or incomplete treatment significantly increases scarring risk.

Unvaccinated men who contract mumps after puberty face a 30-40% incidence of orchitis. MMR vaccination reduces mumps incidence by over 95% and virtually eliminates mumps orchitis as a cause of azoospermia in vaccinated populations.

Men in TB-endemic regions with a past diagnosis of pulmonary TB have a 2-4% rate of male genital TB in older autopsy and clinical series. Early TB treatment reduces genital involvement but does not reverse established epididymal scarring.

riskFactors.factor: Multiple sexual partners without barrier contraception
riskFactors.description: Increases cumulative exposure to C. trachomatis and N. gonorrhoeae. Consistent condom use reduces STI acquisition by 50-90% and is the most effective behavioral prevention measure for infection-related azoospermia.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to STI-endemic regions
riskFactors.description: High-prevalence regions for gonorrhea and chlamydia in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia carry higher rates of untreated STI-related epididymitis and consequent azoospermia, particularly in men with limited healthcare access.

HIV infection and immunosuppressive medications reduce the ability to contain genitourinary infections, allowing more extensive epididymal and testicular damage from pathogens that would ordinarily be contained by an intact immune response.

affectedDemographics: Men aged 20-40 during peak sexual activity years are at highest risk for STI-related epididymitis and its downstream infertility consequence.

Men in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia have substantially higher rates of gonorrhea-related epididymitis and azoospermia, reflecting high background STI prevalence and limited access to early treatment.
Unvaccinated men born before routine MMR vaccination programs represent a cohort at residual risk for mumps orchitis-related azoospermia in high-income countries.
Men from TB-endemic countries — India, Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe — carry background risk of genitourinary TB as a cause of silent epididymal obstruction.
diagnosisOverview: Azoospermia is diagnosed by finding no sperm on two semen analyses using the WHO 2021 reference criteria — centrifuging the whole ejaculate and examining the pellet under phase-contrast microscopy. Once azoospermia is confirmed, the clinical workup distinguishes obstructive from non-obstructive etiology, because treatment differs fundamentally. Serum FSH, LH, and testosterone are measured: FSH above 7.6 IU/L (or double the upper limit of normal) strongly suggests primary spermatogenic failure (non-obstructive pattern), while normal FSH with a normal-volume ejaculate suggests obstruction. Physical examination looks for testicular volume (measured by orchidometer, normal ≥15 mL per testis), epididymal fullness or nodularity suggesting proximal obstruction, and vas deferens palpation. Scrotal ultrasound identifies epididymal dilation, echogenic epididymal changes consistent with fibrosis, intratesticular calcifications from prior tuberculosis, or dilated rete testis. Transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) is performed when ejaculatory duct obstruction is suspected — showing dilated seminal vesicles (>1.5 cm in width) and a midline prostatic cyst or calcification. Genetic testing (karyotype, AZF microdeletion analysis) rules out chromosomal causes of NOA even when infection is suspected, since co-existing genetic causes alter prognosis for sperm retrieval. In cases with a suggestive history, NAAT for C. trachomatis and N. gonorrhoeae from urethral swab or first-voided urine and a urine TB culture (three early-morning specimens) exclude ongoing or latent infection. Diagnostic testicular biopsy confirms the histological pattern (obstructive pattern shows normal spermatogenesis; NOA pattern shows hypospermatogenesis, maturation arrest, or Sertoli-cell-only pattern) and simultaneously allows sperm retrieval for cryopreservation.

diagnosticTests.test: Semen analysis (WHO 2021 criteria, two samples 7+ days apart)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms azoospermia by demonstrating absence of sperm in centrifuged ejaculate pellet
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient produces a semen sample after 2-7 days of sexual abstinence; the lab processes and examines it within 1 hour. Both samples must confirm no sperm under high-power microscopy to diagnose azoospermia. Results available same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes obstructive from non-obstructive azoospermia and assesses Leydig cell function
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A morning fasting blood draw. FSH above twice the upper limit of normal indicates spermatogenic failure (NOA). Normal FSH with azoospermia suggests obstruction. Results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies epididymal dilation, fibrosis, and testicular parenchymal changes indicative of prior infection or obstruction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel-coupled ultrasound probe is applied to the scrotum; no discomfort. The sonographer measures testicular volume, evaluates the epididymis for dilation or echogenic changes, and identifies calcifications from prior TB. Takes about 20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects seminal vesicle dilation and ejaculatory duct obstruction from post-infectious fibrosis or calcification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ultrasound probe placed rectally for 10-15 minutes. Seminal vesicle width above 1.5 cm and dilated ejaculatory ducts support distal obstruction. TRUS can be combined with seminal vesicle aspiration for culture.

diagnosticTests.test: NAAT for C. trachomatis and N. gonorrhoeae
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects active or recent genitourinary infection that may be causing or contributing to epididymal obstruction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: First-voided urine or urethral swab; processed by nucleic acid amplification. Sensitivity >95%. Results within 24-48 hours. Active infection requires treatment before any surgical reconstruction.

diagnosticTests.test: Testicular biopsy with simultaneous sperm extraction (TESE)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Histologically confirms spermatogenesis and retrieves sperm for IVF-ICSI when non-obstructive azoospermia is suspected
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A day-case procedure under local anesthesia or light sedation; a small incision is made in the scrotal skin and testicular tissue is sampled. Sperm retrieved are immediately assessed by an embryologist and can be cryopreserved. Mild discomfort for 2-3 days afterward.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of infection-related azoospermia follows two distinct pathways depending on whether the cause is obstructive or non-obstructive. For obstructive azoospermia (OA) from prior epididymitis, vasoepididymostomy — microsurgical anastomosis of the vas deferens to an upstream undamaged portion of the epididymis — is the gold-standard reconstruction procedure when spermatogenesis is confirmed on biopsy and the obstruction is at the epididymal level. Patency rates with microsurgery are 60-87% and natural pregnancy rates range from 25-60% depending on obstruction duration, partner age, and surgical skill. Vasovasostomy is performed when the vas deferens itself is obstructed. Ejaculatory duct obstruction from calcification or cysts is treated by transurethral resection of the ejaculatory ducts (TURED), which restores antegrade sperm flow in 50-65% of cases. When surgical reconstruction is not feasible (longstanding obstruction >15 years, partner age over 38, or failed reconstruction), percutaneous epididymal sperm aspiration (PESA), testicular sperm aspiration (TESA), or microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) retrieve sperm for in vitro fertilization with intracytoplasmic sperm injection (IVF-ICSI). For non-obstructive azoospermia from mumps orchitis, micro-TESE under optical magnification identifies residual foci of sperm production within the testis, achieving sperm retrieval rates of 30-50%. Active infection — ongoing STI or reactivated TB — must be treated with appropriate antibiotics before any surgical intervention: fluoroquinolones for bacterial epididymitis, anti-tubercular four-drug therapy (HRZE for 2 months then HR for 4 months) for genitourinary TB. Treatment of active infection does not reverse established fibrotic obstruction but prevents further damage.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10-14 days (chlamydial epididymitis)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic for chlamydial epididymitis in the reproductive-age male. When given within the first 7 days of acute epididymitis, early treatment reduces epididymal fibrosis and preserves ductal patency in the majority of cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological cure of C. trachomatis in >95%; prevention of obstructive sequelae highest when started within 7 days of onset

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose plus doxycycline 100 mg twice daily 10-14 days (gonococcal epididymitis)
medicalTreatments.description: CDC 2021 recommended dual therapy for gonococcal epididymitis: ceftriaxone covers gonorrhea; doxycycline covers concurrent chlamydia. Critical to treat both partners simultaneously to prevent re-infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological cure >95% for susceptible gonorrhea; clinical resolution of acute epididymitis within 5-7 days

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-tuberculosis therapy (isoniazid + rifampicin + pyrazinamide + ethambutol for 2 months, then isoniazid + rifampicin for 4 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard 6-month four-drug WHO regimen for genitourinary tuberculosis. Halts progressive epididymal and testicular destruction from TB. Does not reverse established calcified fibrosis but prevents new damage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bacteriological cure >95% for drug-susceptible TB; genital lesion response rates 60-80% on imaging in responsive cases

medicalTreatments.name: Empirical antioxidant supplementation (vitamin C, vitamin E, coenzyme Q10)
medicalTreatments.description: Oxidative stress generated during infection impairs spermatogenesis. Small RCTs support antioxidant supplementation improving sperm DNA fragmentation index and motility in men with non-obstructive azoospermia undergoing sperm retrieval, though evidence for azoospermia specifically is limited.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show modest improvement in sperm parameters in oligozoospermia; role in azoospermia recovery is uncertain

surgicalOptions.name: Vasoepididymostomy (microsurgical epididymovasostomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Microsurgical anastomosis reconnecting the vas deferens to a patent epididymal tubule proximal to the obstruction. Performed under high magnification (×16-25 operating microscope). Requires finding motile sperm in the epididymal fluid at the anastomosis site intraoperatively.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patency rates 60-87%; natural pregnancy rates 25-60% depending on obstruction duration and partner age. Best outcomes when obstruction is less than 5 years old.

surgicalOptions.name: Transurethral resection of ejaculatory ducts (TURED)
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic unroofing of calcified or fibrotic ejaculatory ducts to restore antegrade sperm flow. Performed under spinal or general anesthesia; a resectoscope removes obstructing tissue from the verumontanum region.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Sperm appearance in ejaculate in 50-65% of selected cases; natural pregnancy rates approximately 20-30% in published series

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous epididymal sperm aspiration (PESA)
surgicalOptions.description: A fine needle is inserted into the epididymis under local anesthesia and fluid containing sperm is aspirated for IVF-ICSI. Suitable for obstructive azoospermia when microsurgery is declined or has failed; minimal scarring if performed by experienced andrologist.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Sperm retrieval in 80-95% of obstructive azoospermia cases; fertilization rates 40-60% with ICSI per cycle

surgicalOptions.name: Microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE)
surgicalOptions.description: The testis is opened and examined under high-power magnification (×20-25 operating microscope) to identify enlarged seminiferous tubules containing residual spermatogenesis. The gold standard for non-obstructive azoospermia including post-orchitis cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Sperm retrieval in 30-50% of non-obstructive azoospermia cases; superior to conventional TESE by approximately 30% in meta-analyses

alternativeTreatments.name: Varicocele repair in men with concurrent varicocele and NOA
alternativeTreatments.description: Microsurgical varicocele ligation has been shown to restore sperm to the ejaculate in 20-40% of azoospermic men with a clinical-grade varicocele and testicular histology showing hypospermatogenesis; a 2019 meta-analysis confirmed return of sperm in 40% vs 15% with observation. Applicable when infection is not the sole cause.

whySeeSpecialist: Urological andrologists with subspecialty training in male infertility should evaluate all azoospermic men, as distinguishing obstructive from non-obstructive subtypes and selecting between microsurgical reconstruction and assisted reproduction requires clinical expertise and intraoperative decision-making.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a urological andrologist affiliated with a licensed IVF/ART center and with documented experience in microsurgical vasoepididymostomy (minimum 20 cases per year) and micro-TESE. The best outcomes for couples require simultaneous access to an experienced embryology laboratory for real-time sperm assessment and cryopreservation.
hospitalCriteria: Acute bilateral epididymo-orchitis with systemic sepsis requiring intravenous antibiotics and hospitalisation
Micro-TESE or vasoepididymostomy requiring general anesthesia and intraoperative frozen-section histology
Complication of scrotal surgery — hematoma, wound infection, or testicular ischemia
Active genitourinary tuberculosis with systemic features requiring monitored multi-drug therapy initiation
keyFacilities: Male infertility and andrology clinics with microsurgical operating capability
Licensed IVF centres with andrology and embryology laboratory
Sexual health clinics for STI NAAT testing and partner notification
TB reference centres for microbiological confirmation and drug-sensitivity testing
preventionStrategies: Get vaccinated against mumps via MMR vaccine — the most effective single intervention to prevent viral orchitis-related azoospermia.
Treat chlamydial and gonococcal epididymitis within 7 days of onset with guideline-recommended dual antibiotic therapy to minimize epididymal scarring.
Use condoms consistently with new or casual partners to prevent STI acquisition; consistent condom use reduces chlamydia and gonorrhea transmission by 50-90%.
Screen sexually active men under 25 for chlamydia annually per CDC and USPSTF recommendations, as asymptomatic infection can progress to epididymitis.
In TB-endemic regions, treat active pulmonary or lymph node tuberculosis early with complete 6-month therapy to prevent hematogenous seeding of the epididymis.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full prescribed antibiotic course for acute epididymitis; stopping early when symptoms improve is the most common cause of fibrotic sequelae.
Abstain from sexual activity until both partners have completed treatment for chlamydial or gonococcal epididymitis to prevent re-infection.
Avoid tobacco and alcohol, which independently increase sperm DNA fragmentation and impair the testicular response to infection-related inflammation.
Maintain scrotal temperature at physiological levels: avoid tight underwear, prolonged hot baths, and laptop use on the lap during the post-infection recovery period.
Seek fertility evaluation 6-12 months after completing antibiotic treatment for bilateral epididymitis to determine whether obstruction has occurred and whether surgical correction is feasible.

recommended: Foods rich in antioxidants — lycopene (tomatoes), vitamin C (citrus), vitamin E (nuts, seeds) — to reduce reactive oxygen species damaging spermatogenesis during recovery
Zinc-containing foods (oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds) to support spermatogenesis; deficiency is associated with oligozoospermia
Adequate omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish — DHA is incorporated into sperm membranes and supports motility
Folate-rich foods (legumes, leafy greens) to reduce sperm DNA fragmentation
avoid: Tobacco smoking, which increases sperm DNA fragmentation and reactive oxygen species by approximately 2-fold
Excessive alcohol (more than 14 units/week), associated with reduced testosterone and impaired spermatogenesis
Anabolic steroids, which suppress LH and FSH, inducing reversible or occasionally irreversible secondary non-obstructive azoospermia

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes/week) supports healthy testosterone levels and general fitness. Scrotal rest and avoidance of cycling or vigorous perineal pressure should be maintained for 4-6 weeks after acute epididymitis or scrotal surgery. High-intensity endurance training (>10 hours/week) may transiently suppress spermatogenesis; balance training with recovery.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed antibiotics at consistent times; set phone reminders to maintain therapeutic levels.
Track ejaculate volume and any changes in seminal fluid appearance after ejaculatory duct treatment.
Attend semen analysis at 3, 6, and 12 months post-treatment to monitor whether azoospermia is resolving.
Maintain hormonal monitoring (FSH, testosterone) after mumps orchitis to detect secondary hypogonadism requiring testosterone replacement.
prognosis: Prognosis for biological fatherhood depends heavily on azoospermia subtype and treatment timing. For obstructive azoospermia from unilateral chlamydial epididymitis treated promptly, natural fertility may be preserved. When bilateral obstruction is established, vasoepididymostomy achieves patency in 60-87% of cases, with natural pregnancy rates of 25-60% at 2 years — best when obstruction is less than 5 years old and the partner is under 35. PESA combined with IVF-ICSI offers an alternative with sperm retrieval rates above 90% and cumulative pregnancy rates per couple of 40-60% over three cycles. For non-obstructive azoospermia from mumps orchitis, micro-TESE retrieves sperm in 30-50% of attempts; ICSI pregnancy rates per cycle are 30-45%. Men in whom no sperm is found on micro-TESE face a definitive prognosis of biological infertility; donor sperm insemination or adoption remain family-building options. Testosterone function (Leydig cell) is often preserved even when spermatogenesis is destroyed, so hypogonadism is not an inevitable outcome.
recoveryTimeline: After antibiotic treatment for acute epididymitis, local pain and inflammation resolve over 2-6 weeks. Whether obstruction has developed is assessed at 3-6 months by semen analysis. Surgical reconstruction results can be assessed at 6-12 months post-surgery by semen analysis. Micro-TESE and IVF cycles take 3-6 months per attempt; multiple cycles may be needed.
complications: Permanent bilateral epididymal obstruction causing irreversible obstructive azoospermia — the dominant long-term consequence of untreated bilateral chlamydial or gonococcal epididymitis.
Testicular atrophy and secondary hypogonadism from severe bilateral mumps orchitis, requiring testosterone replacement therapy.
Chronic epididymo-orchitis and scrotal pain from incompletely treated or recurrent bacterial epididymitis, persisting for months after microbiological cure.
Sperm DNA fragmentation and impaired ICSI outcomes from oxidative stress generated by genitourinary infection, even when sperm are successfully retrieved.
Failed vasoepididymostomy requiring conversion to sperm retrieval and IVF-ICSI — approximately 15-40% of microsurgical reconstructions fail to achieve patency.

supportResources.name: American Society for Reproductive Medicine (ASRM) — Male Infertility
supportResources.description: Patient guides on male infertility evaluation, azoospermia subtypes, and assisted reproduction options.

supportResources.name: European Association of Urology — Male Infertility Guidelines
supportResources.description: Evidence-based EAU clinical guidelines on diagnosis and treatment of azoospermia including infection-related causes.

supportResources.name: Resolve: The National Infertility Association
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy and support network for couples facing infertility, including peer support groups and provider directories.

confusedWithConditions.name: Organic Azoospermia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Organic azoospermia encompasses all structural causes including congenital absence of the vas deferens, post-vasectomy, and infection-related obstruction. Infection-related azoospermia specifically requires a documented infectious history or microbiological evidence and guides different counseling about STI prevention. The distinction matters for genetic counseling, as congenital causes imply CFTR mutation testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Orchitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Orchitis is the acute inflammatory phase of testicular infection — presenting with fever, testicular pain, and swelling — and can be a precursor to azoospermia. Azoospermia is the long-term consequence of severe bilateral orchitis, detected months to years later on semen analysis. Orchitis requires acute treatment; azoospermia requires fertility evaluation and reconstruction or ART planning.

confusedWithConditions.name: Male Infertility
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Male infertility is a broad diagnostic category encompassing azoospermia, oligozoospermia, asthenozoospermia, and combined defects. Azoospermia due to infection is a specific etiology within this category. Identifying the infection-related cause allows targeted treatment (antibiotic therapy, microsurgical reconstruction) rather than empirical management.

relatedConditions.name: Azoospermia due to Obstruction of Efferent Ducts
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the obstructive mechanism; efferent duct obstruction may result from prior infection or congenital stenosis and requires the same microsurgical assessment for reconstruction.

relatedConditions.name: Male Infertility
relatedConditions.relevance: Azoospermia is the most severe presentation within the male infertility spectrum; management pathways converge on ART when surgical reconstruction is not possible.

relatedConditions.name: Epididymitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Epididymitis is the acute infectious precursor to obstructive azoospermia; early treatment of epididymitis is the primary prevention strategy for infection-related azoospermia.

relatedConditions.name: Testicular Atrophy
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe bilateral orchitis and post-infective non-obstructive azoospermia are frequently accompanied by testicular volume reduction; both conditions are managed in the same male fertility clinic setting.

faqs.question: What is azoospermia?
faqs.answer: Azoospermia is the complete absence of sperm in the ejaculate, confirmed on two separate semen analyses. It is the most severe form of male infertility, affecting about 1% of all men and 10-15% of men investigated for infertility. Causes include infections, genetic factors, hormonal disorders, and obstruction of the reproductive ducts.

faqs.question: Can STIs cause azoospermia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chlamydia and gonorrhea can cause epididymitis; if both epididymides are scarred by infection, sperm cannot travel from the testis to the ejaculate, causing obstructive azoospermia. Mumps virus can destroy sperm-producing tubules in the testis when orchitis is severe and bilateral, causing non-obstructive azoospermia.

faqs.question: Is azoospermia permanent?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Obstructive azoospermia from infection can be corrected by microsurgical reconnection of the epididymis (vasoepididymostomy), with patency in 60-87% of cases. When microsurgery is not feasible, sperm can be retrieved from the epididymis or testis for IVF-ICSI. Non-obstructive azoospermia from severe orchitis has more limited options but micro-TESE retrieves sperm in 30-50%.

faqs.question: What symptoms does azoospermia cause?
faqs.answer: Azoospermia itself causes no symptoms — ejaculation and orgasm are normal. Men typically discover azoospermia only when investigated for infertility. Underlying causes such as orchitis or epididymitis may leave residual scrotal pain or small, soft testes, but azoospermia alone produces no pain, hormonal symptoms, or urinary changes.

faqs.question: How is azoospermia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Azoospermia is confirmed by centrifuging the entire ejaculate and finding no sperm under microscopy on two separate samples taken at least 7 days apart. Further workup includes serum FSH (distinguishes obstructive from non-obstructive), scrotal ultrasound, NAAT for STIs, and testicular biopsy to confirm spermatogenesis before surgical planning.

faqs.question: Can azoospermia be treated?
faqs.answer: Yes. Obstructive azoospermia from infection can be corrected by microsurgical vasoepididymostomy or by sperm aspiration combined with IVF-ICSI. Non-obstructive azoospermia from testicular damage is treated by micro-TESE to find residual sperm pockets. Active STI or TB causing ongoing damage should be treated first with antibiotics.

faqs.question: What is the difference between obstructive and non-obstructive azoospermia?
faqs.answer: Obstructive azoospermia means the testis produces sperm normally but a physical blockage prevents them from reaching the ejaculate — infection-related scarring is a common cause. Non-obstructive azoospermia means sperm production itself is impaired, as in mumps orchitis. FSH is normal in obstruction and elevated in non-obstructive cases. Treatment differs fundamentally.

faqs.question: Does mumps cause azoospermia?
faqs.answer: Mumps orchitis in post-pubertal men causes azoospermia in approximately 30-40% of those with severe bilateral orchitis. Unilateral orchitis rarely causes azoospermia. MMR vaccination prevents mumps and virtually eliminates this risk. Men who had bilateral mumps orchitis before MMR vaccination should request a semen analysis if planning a family.

faqs.question: Can azoospermia from infection be reversed?
faqs.answer: Obstructive azoospermia from bilateral epididymal scarring can often be reversed by microsurgical vasoepididymostomy, achieving patency in 60-87% and natural pregnancy rates of 25-60% at two years. Best results occur when obstruction is less than 5 years old. Established non-obstructive damage from viral orchitis is not surgically reversible.

faqs.question: Can a man with azoospermia have children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most men with obstructive azoospermia can father biological children through sperm retrieval combined with IVF-ICSI. Men with non-obstructive azoospermia have a 30-50% chance of sperm retrieval via micro-TESE. Donor sperm insemination or adoption are options when no sperm can be retrieved.

faqs.question: Does chlamydia always cause azoospermia?
faqs.answer: No. Most men with chlamydial epididymitis recover without permanent fertility impairment when treated promptly with doxycycline within 7 days of symptom onset. Azoospermia develops only if bilateral epididymal scarring occurs, typically from recurrent, untreated, or delayed-treatment episodes.

faqs.question: What blood tests diagnose azoospermia?
faqs.answer: Serum FSH distinguishes obstructive from non-obstructive azoospermia — elevated FSH indicates testicular damage. LH and testosterone assess Leydig cell function. Karyotype and Y-chromosome microdeletion testing exclude genetic causes. These tests, combined with semen analysis and scrotal ultrasound, form the standard fertility workup.

faqs.question: Is testicular biopsy needed for azoospermia?
faqs.answer: Testicular biopsy is recommended when non-obstructive azoospermia is suspected to confirm spermatogenesis status and to retrieve sperm for cryopreservation. Biopsy is not required to diagnose obstructive azoospermia when clinical and hormonal findings are consistent. Micro-TESE combines diagnostic biopsy and sperm retrieval in the same procedure.

faqs.question: Does tuberculosis cause azoospermia?
faqs.answer: Genitourinary tuberculosis can infect the epididymis and vas deferens, producing bilateral obstruction and azoospermia. It is a recognized cause in TB-endemic countries. Early anti-TB treatment prevents further damage; established calcified obstruction requires surgical assessment. All men from TB-endemic regions with azoospermia should have TB excluded.

faqs.question: How long after epididymitis can azoospermia develop?
faqs.answer: Azoospermia from bilateral epididymal scarring may develop within months of severe acute bilateral epididymitis or gradually over years of recurrent subclinical infection. A semen analysis 3-6 months after acute bilateral epididymitis reveals whether obstruction has occurred. Earlier treatment reduces but does not eliminate the risk.

faqs.question: What is vasoepididymostomy?
faqs.answer: Vasoepididymostomy is a microsurgical procedure that bypasses a scarred section of the epididymis by connecting the vas deferens directly to a healthy epididymal tubule above the obstruction. Performed under a high-power operating microscope, it restores sperm flow in 60-87% of cases and is the gold-standard surgical treatment for infection-related obstructive azoospermia.

metaDescription: Azoospermia: no sperm in semen caused by infection scarring or orchitis. Symptoms, diagnosis, surgical treatment and fertility options reviewed here.
